coredata.dc:title: Unraveling fluorescent mechanism of biomass-sourced carbon dots based on three major components: Cellulose, lignin, and protein
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Tian-xiang
Zhao, De-fang
Li, Lin
Meng, Yang
Xie, Yu-hui
Feng, Dong
Wu, Feng
Xie, Delong
Liu, Yuxin
Mei, Yi
coredata.dc:description: The complexity of biomass components leads to significant variations in the performance of biomass-based carbon dots (CDs). To shed light on this matter, this study presents a comparative analysis of the fluorescence properties of CDs using pure cellulose, lignin, and protein as models. Three CDs showed different fluorescent properties, resulting from the structure difference and carbonization behavior in the hydrothermal. The relatively gentle thermal degradation of proteins allows the macromolecular structure of amino acids to be preserved. This preservation results in a more regular lattice structure, a larger sp2 domain size, and N-doping, which contribute to the highest quantum yield (QY) of 8.7% of the CDs. In contrast, cellulose undergoes more severe thermal degradation with large amounts of small molecules generated, resulting in the CDs with fewer surface defects, more irregular lattice structures, and lower QY. These results provide a guideline for the design of carbon dots from different biomass.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Structure-properties relationship
Surface oxidation
Graphitization
Ion detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs and its applications
Bulk characterization of composition and optical properties of carbon dots
Mechanism discussion behind the optical properties
Optical detection of the heavy metal ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Kunming University of Science and Technology Co-foundation
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China Grant (No. 21663015 and 51603096 ), Yunnan Province – Kunming University of Science and Technology Co-foundation Grant (No. 202101BE070001-034 ), and Engineering Research Center of Biodegradable Polymers, Educational Commission of Yunnan Province (No. KKPU202205001).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–visible absorption spectrum of SPI-CDs (a), LI-CDs (b) and CE-CDs (c); fluorescence spectrum of SPI-CDs (d), LI-CDs (e) and CE-CDs (f); particle size distribution diagram of SPI-CDs (g), LI-CDs (h) and CE-CDs (i). SPI-CDs: soy protein isolate-based carbon dots, LI-CDs: lignin-based carbon dots, and CE-CDs: cellulose-based carbon dots.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy of SPI-CDs, LI-CDs and CE-CDs (a); TGA/DTG curves of soy protein isolate, lignin and cellulose (b) and their enlarge curves from 40 to 180 ℃ (c); X-ray electron spectroscopy full spectrum of SPI-CDs (d), LI-CDs (e) and CE-CDs (f); Schematic diagram of O and N defects of SPI-CDs (g), LI-CDs (h), and CE-CDs (i). SPI-CDs: soy protein isolate-based carbon dots, LI-CDs: lignin-based carbon dots, and CE-CDs: cellulose-based carbon dots.

The structure of carbon dots revealed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM images of SPI-CDs (a), LI-CDs (b), CE-CDs (c); and high resolution TEM of SPI-CDs (d), LI-CDs (e), and CE-CDs (f); Raman of SPI-CDs(g), LI-CDs(h), and CE-CDs (i). SPI-CDs: soy protein isolate-based carbon dots, LI-CDs: lignin-based carbon dots, and CE-CDs: cellulose-based carbon dots.

Three-dimensional Thermogravimetric-Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (TG-FTIR) spectra of SPI (a), LI (d) and CE (g); The FTIR spectrum of SPI (b), LI (e) and CE (h) overflow gases at 180℃; and absorbance of the overflow gases with temperature during the pyrolysis of SPI (c), LI (f) and CE (i). SPI: soy protein isolate, LI: lignin, and CE: cellulose.

Schematic diagram of carbon dot formation of SPI-CDs(a), LI-CDs(b) and CE-CDs(c) in hydrothermal processing. SPI-CDs: soy protein isolate-based carbon dots, LI-CDs: lignin-based carbon dots, and CE-CDs: cellulose-based carbon dots. PLQY: photoluminescence quantum yield.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of SPI-CDs (λex/λem = 360/430 nm) with various metal ions (Ba2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Cr3+ and Fe3+ at 500 µM). (b) SPI-CDs of ion selectivity. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of SPI-CDs (λex/λem = 360/430 nm) with different concentrations of Fe3+ ion ranging from 0.01 to 500 µM. (d) Calibration curve constructed between the values of F0/F and concentration of Fe3+ ion (0.01–500 μM) for SPI-CDs. SPI-CDs: soy protein isolate-based carbon dots.

The element composition of the soy protein isolate-based carbon dots (SPI-CDs), lignin-based carbon dots (LI-CDs), and cellulose-based carbon dots (CE-CDs) from X-ray electron spectroscopy analysis. For each column, all values showed significant different among according to ANOVA (single factor) analysis (p ≤ 0.05). The values are expressed as mean with standard error (n = 2).

The area of graphitized carbon peaks (I(G)) and disordered carbon peaks (I(D)) and their ratios for soy protein isolate-based carbon dots (SPI-CDs), lignin-based carbon dots (LI-CDs), and cellulose-based carbon dots (CE-CDs) obtained from Raman spectrum. For each column, I(D)/I(G) values followed by the same letter are not significant different among according to ANOVA (single factor) analysis (p ≤ 0.05). I(D)/I(G) values are expressed as mean with standard error (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Unraveling fluorescent mechanism of biomass-sourced carbon dots based on three major components: Cellulose, lignin, and protein

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yunnan Provincial Key Laboratory of Energy Saving in Phosphorus Chemical Engineering and New Phosphorus Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The complexity of biomass components leads to significant variations in the performance of biomass-based carbon dots (CDs). To shed light on this matter, this study presents a comparative analysis of the fluorescence properties of CDs using pure cellulose, lignin, and protein as models. Three CDs showed different fluorescent properties, resulting from the structure difference and carbonization behavior in the hydrothermal. The relatively gentle thermal degradation of proteins allows the macromolecular structure of amino acids to be preserved. This preservation results in a more regular lattice structure, a larger sp2 domain size, and N-doping, which contribute to the highest quantum yield (QY) of 8.7% of the CDs. In contrast, cellulose undergoes more severe thermal degradation with large amounts of small molecules generated, resulting in the CDs with fewer surface defects, more irregular lattice structures, and lower QY. These results provide a guideline for the design of carbon dots from different biomass.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) is a 0-dimensional fluorescent carbon material, which exhibits excellent photoluminescence, low toxicity, good optical stability, and biocompatibility (Xu et al., 2020). As a novel nano quantum dots material with quasi-spherical shape and particle size less than 10 nm, it is an ideal material to replace inorganic quantum dots applied to different fields such as biomedicine, real-time monitoring (Si et al., 2022), Light Emitting Diode (LED) device (Xu et al., 2023), non-solar cells (Briscoe et al., 2015), sensors, catalysis, and information encryption and anti-counterfeiting (Rasal et al., 2021). The discovery of CDs was traced back to 2004, in which year the fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNDs) were accidentally observed by Xu et al. during the preparation and purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (Xu et al., 2004). In 2006, Sun et al. successfully synthesized polychromatic fluorescent CNDs by laser ablation method and named them “carbon quantum dots”(Sun et al., 2006). Since then, CDs have drawn more and more attention. At present, various methods for the preparation of CDs have been developed, which can be generally divided into “top-down” and “bottom-up”, such as arc discharging, electrochemical, and hydrothermal/ solvothermal pyrolysis (Chen et al., 2023). Among them, hydrothermal/ solvothermal synthesis has gained widespread acceptance due to its versatility, cost-effectiveness, and simplicity.

Hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment of different biomass such as rice husk (Sun et al., 2020), Miscanthus (Liu et al., 2012), algae (Ramanan et al., 2016), various juice (Sahu et al., 2012) to produce CDs with different fluorescence properties has been reported. However, the reproducibility is a big issue. Even with same biomass and synthetic conditions, the prepared CDs showed different properties. For example, CDs synthesized by Zhao and Sun et al., using garlic as the carbon source through hydrothermal reaction, showed quite different fluorescence quantum yield (QY) (Sun et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2015). This is resulted from the different composition ratio of feedstocks. The composition ratio of cellulose, lignin, and protein can be influenced by the growing season, collecting methods/places and storage methods (O'Reilly, 2022). It is important to consider this reproducibility to ensure the consistency of fluorescence properties made from biomass for the large-scale production.

Besides the different composition ratios, the unclear specific fluorescence mechanism of CDs from biomass exacerbates this reproducibility issues. The photoelectric properties related to the energy band gap of HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbital) - LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) can be adjusted by introducing different type and contents of heteroatoms. The early synthesized CDs mainly contain elements C, H, O, with surface functional hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. However, with the in-depth study of CDs, it is found that the introduction of some heteroatoms (such as P, S, N, etc.) into the skeleton of CDs can make the carbon skeleton form defects, thereby regulating its luminescence performance (Dsouza et al., 2021). Large numbers of biomass itself contains rich heteroatoms, which realize self-doping during hydrothermal processing. The self-doping is believed to be one of the main reasons for the distinct properties exhibited by the CDs. Unfortunately, tailoring the physiochemical properties of CDs is still challenged, because of the unclear mechanism in the preparation process. Therefore, to better control the properties of CDs from biomass, it is necessary to understand the role of different components in the preparation process of CDs.

To reveal the mechanism behind the different physiochemical properties, three kinds of CDs from cellulose (CE), lignin (LI), and soybean protein isolate (SPI) as pure biomass components were prepared by hydrothermal processing. The three different pure biomasses were selected for that cellulose and lignin are the major components of most biomass which showed different chemical structures and properties. Protein, which is not the major component, but previous studies suggested that the nitrogen in protein contribute significantly to the fluorescence properties of the CDs. Soybean protein isolate is a complete protein that provides all types of essential amino acids. Therefore, it was used as a protein model here. The fluorescence properties of CDs were characterized, and the structure-properties relationship were studied herein. Except for the N self-doping, the studies revealed that lattice morphology, graphitization, and surface oxidation were also different in the three CDs, working together to affect the photophysical properties. It is suggested that the composition ratio of the biomass should be stated in future work for better controlling the photophysical properties of the CDs.

Soybean protein isolate (dispersed, 98 %) was purchased from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (China). cellulose (particle size ≤ 25 μm, 98 %) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Inc., (Shanghai, China). Lignin is prepared in laboratory through a sulfate pulping process with a purity of 97 % to 99 %, and all chemicals are used directly without further purification. The deionized water is produced using the Millipore milliq Plus ultra-pure water system (TKA-Gen Pure).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and its applications
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CDs from cellulose, lignin and soy protein isolate was carried out as follows (see Supplementary materials): A mixture of 0.5 g of soy protein isolate powder in 30 mL of distilled water was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel high-pressure reaction autoclave. The autoclave was sealed tightly and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 3 h. This temperature and duration were chosen for their relatively mild conditions, which were previously employed for the preparation of various biomass-based CDs (John et al., 2020), including high quality grass-based CDs (Liu et al., 2012). After cooling, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 10,000 RPM for 30 min to remove any unreacted biomass. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter, and the filtrate was subsequently transferred into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 3500 Da for 24 h of dialysis. Finally, the solution in the dialysis bag was collected, and the CDs of soybean protein isolate, referred to as SPI-CDs (soy-protein isolate based CDs), were obtained by freeze-drying. The prepared CDs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further analysis. Lignin-based carbon quantum dots and cellulose-based carbon quantum dots were prepared using the same method as described above and designated as LI-CDs (Lignin-based CDs) and CE-CDs (cellulose-based CDs), respectively.

The ion detection applications of CDs for various ions were conducted as follows: Initially, 20 mL of CDs was diluted into 100 mL using deionized water to create a standard solution. Subsequently, 1 mL of the standard solution was extracted and combined with 2 mL of a metal ion solution (Ba2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, and Fe3+, each at a concentration of 500 µM, and Fe3+ ion solution at a concentration of 0.01, 10, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500 µM), allowing the mixture to stand for 2 h. The fluorescence intensity (F) at 430 nm (SPI-CDs), 436 nm (LI-CDs), and 445 nm (CE-CDs) under excitation at 360 nm was then measured. The baseline fluorescence intensity (F0) was determined by measuring the fluorescence after combining 1 mL of the standard solution with 2 mL of water.

The optical properties of the synthesized CDs were characterized by UV–visible absorption spectrum (UV–Vis, Shimadzu UV-2250, Japan) scanning from 200 to 600 nm and processed via “M. Wave Professional” software. The fluorescence emission (PL) spectra was collected at an interval of 1 nm from 200 to 600 nm using RF-6000 PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Ultra-pure water was used as the solvent. The organic functional groups of the synthesis CDs were characterized by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) (Nicolet 5700) in the transmission mode at the wavelength range of 400–––4000 cm−1 with the resolution of 4 cm−1. The three CDs were respectively mixed with KBr powders and pressed into a disk for FTIR measurement.

The X-ray diffraction patterns were measured using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Anton Paar XRDynamic500, Germany). The scanning speed was 10°/min from 10 to 80° with the voltage set as 40 KV, and the tube current of 40 mA. The morphological of the CDs were captured by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEM-2100F (JEOL, Japan)). The particle size distribution of CDs was analyzed using Nano Measurer 1.2 software with at least 50 particles were collected. The Raman spectra of the synthesized CDs were scanned from 50 to 3000 cm−1 using a Lab Ram HR Evolution (HORIBA Scientific, France) spectrometer with 532 nm as the excitation light. X-ray electron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested on a Themo Kalpna (thermos scientific, USA). The X-ray source is monochromatic Al Kα with a hυ = 1486.6 eV photo energy operated at 6 mA × 12 kV.

The thermal decomposition behavior of the three biomasses (lignin, cellulose, and soy protein isolate) was studied by a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, STA 449 F3, NETZSCH, Germany). The biomass was heated from 25 to 500 °C at 10 °C/min under Nitrogen protection. To further explore the structural changes of biomass raw materials during the hydrothermal carbonization process, the three different biomasses was heated in a PerkinElmer thermogravimetric analyzer and the in-situ IR spectra of the gas degraded from the biomass was synchronously collected by a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer (TGA-FTIR). The biomass was heated from 25 to 180 °C at 10 °C/min. 50 mL/min nitrogen gas was used as a protective gas. Accordingly, in situ IR spectra were collected with a resolution of 2 cm−1 from 400 to 4000 cm−1.

The quantum yield (QY) of carbon quantum dots was measured by relative method. Quinine sulfate is dissolved in 0.05 M sulfuric acid solution, and its QY is 55 % when excited at 350 nm. It can be used as a standard for measuring QY termination by Eq. (1) (Bhamore et al., 2018).
(1)
Φ x
=
Φ s
∙
F x
F s
∙
A s
A x
∙
η x
η s
where, Φs and Φx represents the QY of standards and CDs. F, A, η represent fluorescence intensity, absorbance, and refractive index of the solvents at excitation wavelength respectively. F is calculated from the area S of the fluorescence emission peak in the range of 480 ∼ 620 nm.

Differences in results were compared by one-way factor analysis of variance (ANOVA) by Data-analysis tool in Microsoft Excel Worksheet 2017 at the p < 0.05 showing the significance level. Additionally, linear regression was applied to fit the metal ion detection model, while the determination coefficient (R2) analysis was applied to reveal the linear correlation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bulk characterization of composition and optical properties of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of CDs utilizing soy protein isolate, lignin, and cellulose as carbon sources was initially confirmed through the observation of a color change from brown in daylight to blue under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 1 a-c). Further investigation into their optical properties by UV-Vis spectrum revealed distinct absorption peaks in each of the three types of CDs. The absorption peaks at 209 nm, 224 nm, and 228 nm in these CDs correspond to the π-π* transition of the conjugated structure in the carbon skeleton. In contrast, the absorption peaks at 272 nm, 275 nm, and 280 nm primarily result from n-π* transitions of functional groups containing oxygen and nitrogen (Jia et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2020). Notably, the intensity of the two absorption peaks differs significantly among the three types of CDs. CE-CDs exhibit a much stronger ultraviolet absorption peak for n-π* transition (280 nm) but a lower absorption peak at 228 nm, while the other two types exhibit a strong absorption peak at ∼ 209 nm and a weak shoulder at ∼ 273 nm. This suggests that the much stronger n-π* transition was mainly resulted from the presence of C = O structures, favored by the abundance of polyhydroxy groups in cellulose. Therefore, it suggested that the n-π* transition in CDs derived from biomass primarily arises from the cellulose or hemicellulose components.

This different excitation wavelength dependence comes from the difference in energy levels. The presence of various C-OH and C-O-C functional groups in CE-CDs results in local electronic states related to epoxide (or OH) below the π* state, leading to new energy levels within n–π* gaps, causing lower excitation energies and consequently a longer excitation wavelength (Liao et al., 2020). This is reflected by the wavelength-dependent excitation of the three CDs with increasing excitation wavelength from 280 to 440 nm. The CE-CDs exhibit limited or no excitation at short wavelengths (280 nm, 300 nm), as revealed by the fluorescence emission spectra of the three CDs in Fig. 1(d-f). The maximum excitation wavelength also varies slightly. CE-CDs have a maximum excitation wavelength at 340 nm, while SPI-CDs and LI-CDs have it at 360 nm. Although they all demonstrated similar red shift wavelength-dependent excitation.

The photoluminescence QY of the CDs was further calculated and found to be 8.7 % for SPI-CDs, the highest, 4.0 % for LI-CDs, and 2.2 % for CE-CDs. This implies that higher protein content in biomass leads to a higher QY. As previously reported, a comparative study showed that the different QY of the CDs made from roasted mutton and serum protein (BSA) was resulted from the different nitrogen doping (Wang et al., 2015). A higher QY results in better luminous efficiency. As shown in Fig. 1, the brightness of SPI-CDs is significantly higher. The prepared SPI-CDs show a QY compared to previously reported CDs from different carbon sources, such as the grass-based CDs (Liu et al., 2012). Therefore, the SPI-CDs hold promise for applications such as ion sensing, biological imaging, catalysis, and other fields.

The particle sizes of the three types of CDs were determined through a statistical analysis of TEM images, as shown in Fig. 1 (g-i). The particle size distribution for all three CDs fell within the range of 1 to 4 nm, with a consistent mean value of 2.5 ± 0.5 nm. This uniformity in diameter suggests that the particle size of the CDs remained largely unaffected by the presence of biomass components when employing the same preparation methods and purification procedures. Additionally, the small particle size (less than 10 nm) confirms the successful synthesis of the CDs.

Unlike other quantum dots, the particle size does not significantly impact the optical properties of these CDs. The optical characteristics of CDs are primarily influenced by factors such as element compositions, the abundance of oxidized surface defects, and the size of sp2 domains (Nguyen et al., 2020). Therefore, there is initially conducted a characterization of the element composition and surface groups in the three different types of CDs.

The functional groups of CDs were characterized through infrared spectroscopy, as depicted in Fig. 2 a. Noticeably, significant differences were observed in the surface properties of the three CDs. For instance, the C-N groups observed in SPI-CDs at 1400 cm−1 were absent in CE-CDs and LI-CDs, a result of the absence of nitrogen in cellulose and lignin. Furthermore, the –OH peaks (at 3367 cm−1) and C-O-C peaks (at 1115 cm−1) of CE-CDs were notably more pronounced compared to those in LI-CDs and SPI-CDs. This suggests a higher presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CE-CDs, which is consistent with the chemical structures and thermal degradation properties of biomass.

The thermal decomposition of the biomass was studied by TGA analysis as the different thermal degradation behavior would cause significant differences in structural evolution during carbonization. The TGA results of the three biomasses was showed in Fig. 2b and 2c and their decomposition temperature was determined (see Supplementary materials). The decomposition happened below 100 ℃ was mainly caused by the loss of the residual moisture in the biomass. TGA results revealed that cellulose was completely decomposed within the entire temperature range (180–500 °C), unlike SPI and LI whose residual were 30.8 % and 21.1 % at 500 °C, respectively (see Supplementary materials). Although the onset decomposition temperature (the temperature of 5 % weight loss) of cellulose was the highest among three, it was suggested that the thermal stability of SPI and LI would be better than that of cellulose during the high-pressure hydrothermal processing. It was expected that SPI will have more main chain residues during hydrothermal decomposition, which influenced the structure and properties of the carbon dots. Moreover, this different thermal degradation behavior will be further discussed by TGA-FTIR analysis in the following section.

Furthermore, XPS was employed to analyze the element composition and surface groups of the three different CDs (Fig. 2d, e, and f). While three CDs exhibited characteristic peaks for C1s (285 eV), N1s (400 eV), and O1s (531 eV), their contents varied significantly. Nitrogen was only found in SPI-CDs, constituting 15.1 % of the composition. Detailed peak fitting of XPS curves (see Supplementary materials) revealed that the nitrogen in SPI-CDs was primarily amide N. This amine nitrogen can significantly enhance the fluorescence QY of CDs (Dong et al., 2012), because the unpaired electrons in nitrogen atoms serve as electron donors, thereby improving the optical properties of CDs (Wu et al., 2019), like the role of carbon atoms (Qi et al., 2015). In addition, the O to C ratio was extracted from XPS data and calculated as 0.71 for SPI-CDs, 1.01 for LI-CDs, and 1.52 for CE-CDs (as shown in Table. 1 ). All values showed significant different among according to ANOVA (single factor) analysis (p ≤ 0.05), indicating the vast difference between elemental contents of the three CDs.

A higher O content implies a greater presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in CE and LI-CDs; however, it doesn't necessarily indicate a higher degree of oxidation. The degree of oxidation can be assessed by the shift in the O1s peak towards lower binding energy (Wang et al., 2020). The greater the deviation, the higher the degree of surface oxidation. LI-CDs exhibited the highest degree of O1s peak shifting (see Supplementary materials), indicating the highest oxidation degree. Surface oxidation introduces defects, which can serve as trapping sites for excitation (Liu et al., 2018). A higher degree of oxidation leads to more defects on the CD surface, resulting in a narrower energy gap and increased QY (Moon et al., 2014). This explains the slightly superior fluorescence performance of LI-CDs compared to CE-CDs. Conversely, the surface oxidation degree of SPI-CDs is lower than that of LI-CDs and CE-CDs, yet their fluorescence performance is outstanding. This is primarily attributed to nitrogen doping rather than surface oxidation. The amino N, located at the edge of the carbon structure or on the carbon skeleton's surface, increases the number of photoexcited electrons in the transition channel, enhances the probability of radiative transition, and greatly boosts the fluorescence intensity of the CDs (Wu et al., 2019).

Graphitized structure characterization. The structures of SPI-CDs, LI-CDs, and CE-CDs were investigated using both Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) and High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM). In the low magnification TEM images depicted in Fig. 3 a-c, it is evident that all three types of CDs are uniformly dispersed, exhibiting quasi-spherical shapes and well-separated distribution from one another. To delve further into the graphitization degree of carbon in these CDs, high magnification TEM images were employed, as presented in Fig. 3d-f. Notably, only SPI-CDs demonstrated distinct and well-defined lattice striations (Fig. 3d) with a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, corresponding to graphite carbon. In the case of LI-CDs, a lattice structure was observed, although it appeared irregularly arranged, and the measured D-spacing was approximately 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of the crystalline region (Li & Dong, 2018). However, for CE-CDs, the crystal lattice morphology appeared less well-defined. Therefore, besides N-doping, it is believed that the presence of a regular lattice striation structure also contributes to the higher QY (QYs) of SPI-CDs, a factor that has often been overlooked in previous research. Most studies have traditionally attributed the fluorescence of CDs to the surface state of carbon atoms, with less attention paid to the lattice structure of carbon nuclei. However, the presence of lattice CDs indicates a high degree of graphitization, fewer edge defects (Mishra et al., 2021), which work together with the N-doping and digree of oxidation to affect the fluorescence dramatically.

Raman spectroscopy is commonly employed to characterize the graphitization and morphology of carbon. In Raman spectra, the peak at 1335 cm−1 (known as the D band) corresponds to defect areas or the disordered structure of carbon materials. Conversely, the peak at 1590 cm−1 (referred to as the G band) represents the sp2 hybrid carbon graphitization structure, providing insight into the size of sp2 domains. A lower I (D) / I (G) value signifies a higher degree of graphitization in the prepared carbon materials (Hou et al., 2015). The Raman spectra of SPI-CDs, LI-CDs, and CE-CDs are displayed in Fig. 3, with the respective I (D) / I (G) ratios provided in Table 2 .

All three CDs displayed both graphitized carbon peaks and disordered carbon peaks, each with distinct I (D) / I (G) ratios. Specifically, the ratios were 0.99, 1.01, and 1.15 for SPI-CDs, LI-CDs, and CE-CDs, respectively. The graphitization degrees of SPI-CDs and LI-CDs were nearly identical according to the ANOVA (single factor) analysis, the P-value was 0.14 which is much larger than 0.05, indicating the values were not significant different. However, the graphitization degrees of CE-CDs were relatively lower with a significant high I (D) / I (G) ratio. This indicates that CE-CDs possess a more disordered structure, aligning with the previous observations from HRTEM images. Importantly, these defects and disordered structures hold potential benefits for the future modification and passivation of CDs (Yuan et al., 2017).

The degree of graphitization was further assessed through XRD analysis, with the corresponding XRD curves of the CDs presented (see Supplementary materials). Analysis of the XRD results indicated a progressive increase in the degree of graphitization from CE-CDs to SPI-CDs (SPI-CDs > LI-CDs > CE-CDs), as evidenced by the heightened intensity of the peak at approximately 23°, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphite carbons (Tammina and Yang, 2020). Notably, the SPI-CDs exhibited a conspicuous graphite peak, while that of LI-CDs was relatively smaller, and CE-CDs displayed almost no discernible graphite peak. This finding aligns consistently with observations from HRTEM and Raman spectra.

The CDs derived from cellulose, lignin, and protein exhibited distinct fluorescence properties, including differences in excitation/emission wavelength dependence, QY, and fluorescence intensity. Upon structural analysis, it was evident that SPI-CDs featured more regular lattice striations in the carbon core and a higher degree of graphitization compared to the CE-CDs. It is believed that these factors, in conjunction with the well-known N-doping, contributed to the higher QY of SPI-CDs. In contrast, CE-CDs exhibited the lowest degree of graphitization but the highest concentration of surface functional groups among the three. The former factor led to the lowest QY of CE-CDs. Although CE-CDs possessed numerous oxygen-containing functional groups, the surface functional groups were relatively uniform, leading to a reduction in various radiative transitions, excitation range, and dependence. The QY of LI-CDs exceeded that of CE-CDs, primarily attributed to the surface oxidation degree of LI-CDs. The heightened surface oxidation degree introduced additional surface defects, which could serve as excitation traps, narrowing the energy gap and increasing the fluorescence QY (Song et al., 2019). To elucidate the mechanisms behind these phenomena, a TGA-FTIR analysis was conducted to unveil the structural and chemical changes that might occur during the production of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism discussion behind the optical properties
ce:section.ce:para: The TGA-FTIR spectra of the three raw materials are displayed in Fig. 4 . Although cellulose exhibited the highest stability at low temperatures (<300℃) among the three in TGA testing, the volatile compounds generated from cellulose during its decomposition were notably complex. This decomposition yielded various gases, including CO2 (2360 cm−1), CO (2180 cm−1), CH4 (3016 cm−1) and H2O (3740 cm−1) unlike soy protein isolate and lignin, which mainly decomposed into CO2. The decomposition of cellulose involved two primary, competitive steps: one attributed to the formation of tars (primarily levoglucosan) and char, and the other involving the generation of light gases, including CO, CO2, and H2O, etc.(Capart et al., 2004). In the case of lignin, a small amount of CO was produced. The release of CO2 and CO during the pyrolysis of lignin resulted from the cracking and reforming of thermolabile carboxyl, carbonyl, and ether groups in the phenylpropane side chains. Notably, the release temperature of CO was much higher than that of CO2 (Wang et al., 2009). The release of CO stemmed from the cleavage of ether-bridges connecting sub-units, characterized by their low dissociation energy of 250–290 kJ/mol (Brebu & Vasile, 2010). Surprisingly, nitrogen-containing compounds, such as amines (970–906 cm−1) and amides, were not generated during the protein's decomposition, indicating that the amino group remained attached to the carbon skeleton during pyrolysis at 180 °C. CO2 was produced as a result of the C-C bond cleavage in the protein chains (Tsukada et al., 1994), aligning with theoretical predictions that carbon–carbon single bonds have lower bond energy than carbon–nitrogen bonds. Previous studies on the hydrothermal treatment of proteins, using silk fibroin as a model material, demonstrated the generation of various amino acids, such as glycine (Gly), alanine (Ala), serine (Ser), and aspartic acid (Asp), with high molecular weight amino acids like Ser and Asp further undergoing decomposition into lower molecular weight amino acids Gly and Ala at a temperature of 250 ℃(Kang & Chun, 2004). Other studies have indicated that the oxidation processes of macromolecular products, such as amino acids, to acetic acid, are highly temperature-dependent and are significantly hindered when the temperature is below 220 °C (Lamoolphak et al., 2008). Thus, based on TGA-FTIR analysis, it is plausible that primarily macromolecular products, such as Gly and Ala, are produced during the decomposition of soy protein isolate.

The hydrothermal decomposition conditions were significantly more complex than those of TGA, as they involved high pressure and water. By combining data with previous research on the mechanisms of thermal decomposition of cellulose, lignin, and protein (Shen & Gu, 2009), it is can illustrate the possible formation mechanism of CDs (CDs) derived from the three different biomasses (Fig. 5 ). In contrast to lignin and protein, cellulose underwent more rapid and extensive decomposition during hydrothermal treatment, as corroborated by TGA-FTIR analysis and prior mechanism studies. Small molecule products, such as Levoglucosan (Xu et al., 2023) were generated along with other gas molecules in the emissions from cellulose pyrolysis. These molecules collided, aggregated, and carbonized to form CE-CDs. This intense degradation likely disrupted the integrity of the cellulose skeleton structure, resulting in the irregular lattice structure observed in the CDs. As a consequence, the graphitization and sp2 domain size of CE-CDs were the lowest among the three. In contrast, protein and lignin were not as easily decomposed into smaller molecules (with CO2 being the primary product). Instead, larger molecules such as benzene ring skeletons or diphenyl ether structures in lignin, or polysized amino acids from protein, directly aggregated and carbonized to form LI-CDs or SPI-CDs. The conjugated functional groups contained in SPI and LI, such as benzene rings in LI, directly formed conjugated carbon cores during the aggregation carbonization process. However, CE found it more challenging to form graphite carbon cores with conjugated structures, as most of the carbon skeleton of CE had decomposed into smaller molecules. This observation aligns with the results of FTIR, UV–Vis, and Raman spectra. In comparison to CE-CDs, which form from small molecules, carbonization from the carbon skeleton results in a more regular lattice structure, as confirmed by HRTEM results. Because –NH2 is not separated during the pyrolysis of SPI at 180 °C, it can combine with carbon chains and become integrated into CDs, introducing self-doping of nitrogen, and consequently, a high QY in the SPI-CDs.

Comparing the properties and formation mechanisms of CDs derived from SPI, CE, and LI, it is evident that CDs differ, even when produced under the same experimental conditions. Natural biomass contains varying proportions of lignin, cellulose, protein, and other substances, and their ratios differ based on cultivation conditions and pretreatment methods. These differences influence the fluorescence properties of the CDs prepared. Consequently, when producing CDs from biomass, it is crucial to consider the composition of its components, addressing these factors in research. Furthermore, the fluorescence properties of the CDs can be customized by adjusting the ratio of the three components, depending on the specific application field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical detection of the heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The application of the prepared CDs was initially conducted for the detection of metal ions in water. The study encompassed the analysis of various ions, including Ba2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, and Fe3+, across three distinct CDs. Fig. 6 illustrates the fluorescence spectra of SPI-CDs (λex/λem = 360/430 nm) concerning different metal ions and its fluorescence intensity in relation to varying Fe3+ concentrations. Meanwhile, the detection performance of the other two CDs, namely CE-CDs and LI-CDs on those ions was also checked (see Supplementary materials). It was observed that all three CDs exhibited heightened sensitivity to Fe3+ compared to other ions. The interaction of CDs with the Fe3+ solution resulted in pronounced fluorescence quenching, signified by a decline in emission intensity at 430 nm (Fig. 6a and 6b). This effect was absent when exposed to other metal ions at same concentration.

To further scrutinize the sensitivity of optical Fe3+ detection by CDs, a series of Fe3+ solutions with concentrations ranging from 0.01 to 500 μM were prepared. Fig. 6d presents a comparative plot of the net change in absorbance of SPI-CDs upon binding with varying concentrations of Fe3+. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) of SPI-CDs demonstrated a proportional relationship with Fe3+ ion concentration, exhibiting linearity with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9879 in the 0.01 to 500 μM range (Fig. 6d). The predicted limit of detection was 63.6 nM from the linear relationship. Notably, SPI-CDs outperformed LI-CDs and CE-CDs (see Supplementary materials) by showcasing lower detection limit values and a broader detection range, attributed to its higher quantum yield. The prepared CDs also demonstrated promising applications in LED technology. The potential for fabricating high-frequency and short-wave blue light emission LEDs using these CDs is evident (see Supplementary materials), and this aspect will be explored and discussed in future research.

The picture that emerges for biomass-based CDs is that the components and their evolution during hydrothermal are responsible for the fluorescence properties. The study underscores the importance of preserving macromolecular structure of amino acid during carbonization in conferring the N-doping, more regular lattice structure, and larger sp2 domain size of the SPI-CDs. The SPI-CDs, therefore, exhibit higher quantum yield and fluorescence intensity compared to the other two. The SPI-CDs exhibited a rather low detection limitation of 63.56 nM Fe3+ with broader detection range compared to that of CE-CDs and LI-CDs, which could be applied as an outstanding optical sensor.

Tian-xiang Li: Writing – original draft, Data curation. De-fang Zhao: Data curation. Lin Li: Data curation. Yang Meng: Methodology. Yu-hui Xie: Methodology, Resources. Dong Feng: Formal analysis. Feng Wu: Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Delong Xie: Funding acquisition, Resources. Yuxin Liu: Methodology. Yi Mei: Funding acquisition, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China Grant (No. 21663015 and 51603096), Yunnan Province – Kunming University of Science and Technology Co-foundation Grant (No. 202101BE070001-034), and Engineering Research Center of Biodegradable Polymers, Educational Commission of Yunnan Province (No. KKPU202205001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2023.130268.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
